docs.title: sPyTial
docs.text: sPyTial turns Python objects into box-and-arrow diagrams backed by Spytial's layout engine. It is aimed at cases where the structure matters more than the UI: debugging recursive objects, teaching data structures, inspecting serialized state, or building small visual tools around Python models.

docs.title: Install
docs.text: pip install spytial-diagramming

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
import spytial

tree = {
    "name": "root",
    "children": [
        {"name": "left"},
        {"name": "right"},
    ],
}

spytial.diagram(tree)

docs.title: What lives in this site
docs.text:
This site focuses on using sPyTial:

  • installation and first steps
  • diagramming and evaluation
  • operations and custom relationalizers
  • worked examples drawn from classic data structures

docs.title: Using spytial-clrs as the examples repo
docs.text:
The sibling spytial-clrs repository is the best source of worked examples for this package. It contains CLRS-style notebooks for linked lists, heaps, trees, graphs, disjoint sets, and memoization tables.

  • It gives you larger, more realistic examples than small dict and list snippets.
  • It shows how sPyTial can be used to explain common data structures visually.
  • It is a good place to browse if you want inspiration for your own diagrams.

Start with Getting Started, then move to Diagramming or CLRS Examples.

docs.title: Dataclass Builder
docs.text: This page has moved to Use sPyTial > Dataclass Builder.

docs.title: Development Guide
docs.text:
This page is no longer part of the published user documentation.

For public docs, use:

  • Getting Started
  • Diagramming
  • Operations

docs.title: Performance Metrics
docs.text:
Performance and headless rendering are now documented in Diagramming.

Use that page for:

  • headless=True
  • perf_iterations
  • perf_path
  • local benchmarking workflows

docs.title: Install
docs.text: pip install spytial-diagramming

docs.title: First diagram
docs.text:
import spytial

example = {
    "id": "root",
    "children": [
        {"id": "left"},
        {"id": "right"},
    ],
}

spytial.diagram(example)

By default, sPyTial renders inline in notebooks and opens a browser tab everywhere else.

docs.title: Inspect before you diagram
docs.text:
If you want to confirm how an object is being serialized, use the evaluator first:

import spytial

spytial.evaluate(example)

This is useful when you are debugging a custom class, an annotation, or a relationalizer.

docs.title: Display methods
docs.text:
sPyTial chooses a display method based on environment:

  • Notebook: renders inline.
  • Anywhere else: opens a browser tab.

You can override this explicitly:

spytial.diagram(example, method="browser")   # open a new tab
spytial.diagram(example, method="file")      # write an HTML file
spytial.diagram(example, method="inline")    # force inline output

docs.title: When to use each tool
docs.text: Tool Best for Output diagram() Visualizing object structure HTML diagram (inline or browser) evaluate() Inspecting serialized data HTML evaluator view dataclass_builder() Constructing dataclass instances Interactive builder

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Read Diagramming for the main rendering workflow.
  • Read Operations to control layout and drawing.
  • Browse CLRS Examples for richer data-structure examples.

docs.title: Operations
docs.text:
Operations define how sPyTial interprets and renders your data. They fall into two categories:

  • Constraints describe layout.
  • Directives describe drawing and presentation.

Most users attach operations to classes with decorators. You can also attach them to specific objects or supply them via typing.Annotated or spytial.AnnotatedType.

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text:
Constraints shape the geometry of the final diagram:

  • orientation: directional relationships between selected boxes
  • align: force selected boxes to share an axis
  • cyclic: arrange selected boxes in a cycle
  • group: place selected boxes inside a labeled bounding region

docs.title: Directives
docs.text:
Directives change what the diagram looks like without changing the structure:

  • atomColor and edgeColor
  • attribute
  • tag
  • inferredEdge
  • icon
  • hideField and hideAtom
  • size

The API Reference lists the full argument surface for each operation.

docs.title: Class decorators
docs.text:
Attach operations to a class using decorators. This is the most common and reusable approach.

import spytial

@spytial.orientation(selector="children", directions=["below"])
@spytial.group(selector="children", name="subtree")
class Node:
    def __init__(self, value, children=None):
        self.value = value
        self.children = children or []

docs.title: Object-level annotations
docs.text:
Attach operations to a single instance without modifying the class:

import spytial

node = Node("root")
spytial.annotate_group(node, selector="children", name="root children")
spytial.annotate_orientation(node, selector="children", directions=["below"])

docs.title: typing.Annotated and AnnotatedType
docs.text:
Use these when you want to annotate plain containers such as dict and list values.

from typing import Dict, List
from spytial import AnnotatedType, InferredEdge, Orientation

Graph = AnnotatedType(
    Dict[int, List[int]],
    InferredEdge(name="edge", selector="values"),
    Orientation(selector="values", directions=["right"]),
)

spytial.diagram({0: [1, 2]}, as_type=Graph)

docs.title: Example directives
docs.text:
The tag directive displays computed values as node attributes without removing the underlying edges:

import spytial

@spytial.tag(toTag="Person", name="age", value="age")
class Person:
    def __init__(self, name, age):
        self.name = name
        self.age = age

Edge appearance can be customized with color, style, weight, and visibility:

import spytial

@spytial.edgeColor(field="parent", value="blue", style="solid", weight=2)
@spytial.edgeColor(field="sibling", value="gray", style="dashed")
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, value, parent=None):
        self.value = value
        self.parent = parent

docs.title: Convenience operations
docs.text:
apply_if lets you conditionally attach operations:

import spytial

@spytial.apply_if(
    lambda cls: cls.__name__.endswith("Node"),
    spytial.atomColor(selector="self", value="lightblue"),
)
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, value):
        self.value = value

docs.title: Patterns used in spytial-clrs
docs.text:
The CLRS notebooks are a good source of idiomatic operation usage:

  • linked lists use attribute(...) to turn fields into compact labels
  • heaps and matrix-style examples use align(...) to create readable rows or sibling levels
  • disjoint sets, hash tables, and SCC views use group(...) to expose higher-level regions

docs.title: Python Integration Details
docs.text:
This page is no longer part of the published user documentation.

For the public docs, use:

  • Operations
  • Custom Relationalizers

docs.title: Custom Relationalizers
docs.text: Relationalizers are plug-ins that teach sPyTial how to serialize your custom objects into atoms and relations.

docs.title: When to use a relationalizer
docs.text:
Use a relationalizer when:

  • you want fine-grained control over how an object becomes nodes and edges
  • your objects are not handled well by the built-in defaults
  • you need domain-specific relations such as depends_on or flows_to

docs.title: Anatomy of a relationalizer
docs.text:
A relationalizer inherits from RelationalizerBase and implements two methods:

  • can_handle(obj) returns True when the relationalizer should handle the object
  • relationalize(obj, walker_func) returns Atom and Relation instances
from spytial import RelationalizerBase, relationalizer, Atom, Relation

@relationalizer(priority=100)
class WidgetRelationalizer(RelationalizerBase):
    def can_handle(self, obj):
        return hasattr(obj, "widget_id")

    def relationalize(self, obj, walker_func):
        atom = Atom(
            id=f"widget:{obj.widget_id}",
            type="Widget",
            label=obj.label,
        )
        return [atom], []

docs.title: Built-in coverage first
docs.text:
Before you write a custom relationalizer, check whether the built-ins already cover your case. spytial-py already ships relationalizers for:

  • primitives
  • dict, list, tuple, and set
  • dataclasses
  • generic Python objects as a fallback

Most examples in spytial-clrs work without custom relationalizers because regular Python objects plus operations are usually enough.

docs.title: Priorities
docs.text: Relationalizers with higher priorities are checked first. Priorities from 0-99 are reserved for built-ins, so custom relationalizers should use 100 or higher.

docs.title: Registering and inspecting
docs.text:
The @relationalizer decorator registers the class automatically when the module is imported. You can inspect the active registry like this:

from spytial import RelationalizerRegistry

print(RelationalizerRegistry.list_relationalizers())

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text: Once your relationalizer is in place, use spytial.evaluate() first to validate the emitted structure, then spytial.diagram() to tune layout and directives.

docs.title: spytial-clrs Examples
docs.text: spytial-clrs is the companion examples repository for spytial-py. It contains notebooks based on CLRS data structures and is the best source of realistic diagrams for docs, demos, and screenshots.

docs.title: Why it is useful
docs.text:
If you want to see what sPyTial looks like on larger structures, spytial-clrs is the best place to start:

  • it provides realistic examples that go beyond toy dict and list inputs
  • it shows how different layout operations help explain specific data structures
  • it gives you a gallery of end-to-end examples you can adapt for your own work

docs.title: Example guide
docs.text:
Use the notebooks as a guide depending on what you want to visualize:

Notebook Structures Good docs topics stacksqueues.ipynb stacks, queues attributes, linear structure, first tutorial examples linked-lists.ipynb singly, doubly, circular linked lists field hiding, attributes, cyclic layout heaps.ipynb max heap, Fibonacci heap recursive structures, alignment, richer edge styling trees.ipynb BST, red-black tree, order statistic tree, interval tree, B-tree, van Emde Boas tree annotations on recursive types, grouped structures, larger gallery examples hash-tables.ipynb direct-address and chained hash tables grouping, selector-heavy examples, labeled fields graphs.ipynb graphs, MST, SCC views graph-like structures, grouping, alternative visual projections disjoint-sets.ipynb disjoint set forests group constraints and forest layouts memoization.ipynb matrix-style DP tables alignment constraints and grid-like layouts

docs.title: How to use these examples
docs.text:
• Start with stacksqueues.ipynb or linked-lists.ipynb if you are new to sPyTial.
  • Browse trees.ipynb and graphs.ipynb for richer layouts and more advanced operation combinations.
  • Use the notebook closest to your own data shape as a template for your first diagram.

docs.title: API Reference
docs.text: This section is generated directly from the sPyTial source code using mkdocstrings.

docs.title: RelationalizerBase
docs.text:
Bases: ABC

Abstract base class for relationalizers.

Relationalizers convert Python objects into atoms and relations for spatial visualization. They define how objects are represented as atoms and how relationships between objects are expressed as spatial relations.

docs.title: can_handle(obj) abstractmethod
docs.text:
Return True if this relationalizer can handle the given object.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default obj Any

The Python object to test

required

Returns:

Type Description bool

True if this relationalizer can process the object, False otherwise

docs.title: relationalize(obj, walker_func) abstractmethod
docs.text:
Convert object to atoms and relations using structured types.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default obj Any

The object to convert

required walker_func Any

Function to recursively walk nested objects

required

Returns:

Name Type Description List[Atom]

Tuple of (atoms_list, relations_list)

atoms_list List[Relation]

List of Atom instances representing the object

relations_list Tuple[List[Atom], List[Relation]]

List of Relation instances connecting this object to others

docs.title: RelationalizerRegistry
docs.text: Registry for relationalizers.

docs.title: register(relationalizer_cls, priority=0) classmethod
docs.text: Register a relationalizer class with given priority.

docs.title: find_relationalizer(obj) classmethod
docs.text: Find the first relationalizer that can handle the given object.

docs.title: list_relationalizers() classmethod
docs.text:
List all registered relationalizers with their priorities.

Returns:

Type Description List[Tuple[int, str]]

List of (priority, relationalizer_class_name) tuples, sorted by priority (highest first)

docs.title: clear() classmethod
docs.text: Clear all registered relationalizers (for testing).

docs.title: SequenceRecorder
docs.text:
Context manager for recording a sequence of object snapshots for visualization.

Uses a single shared builder across all :meth:record calls so that atom IDs remain stable across frames. This handles two common patterns:

  • In-place mutation — the same Python objects are modified between frames. Because id(obj) never changes, the persistent ID table keeps IDs stable automatically with no extra configuration.

  • Snapshot / deepcopy — a fresh copy of the data structure is created each step (e.g. functional algorithms, undo-history). Pass an identity callable that returns a stable string key for each object; atoms will then receive an identity:<key> ID that stays constant across frames.

Example — in-place mutation::

with spytial.sequence() as seq:
    seq.record(root)
    insert(root, 42)
    seq.record(root)
    insert(root, 7)
    seq.record(root)
seq.diagram()

Example — deepcopy snapshots with identity::

with spytial.sequence(identity=lambda n: str(n.id)) as seq:
    for snap in snapshots:
        seq.record(snap)
seq.diagram(method="file")

docs.title: record(obj)
docs.text: Capture a snapshot of obj as the next frame in the sequence.

docs.title: diagram(method=None, auto_open=None, width=None, height=None, title=None, sequence_policy=None)
docs.text:
Render all recorded frames as an interactive sequence visualization.

Parameters override the values supplied to :func:sequence / the constructor; omit them to use the constructor defaults.

docs.title: DataClassBuilder
docs.text: Placeholder that raises on instantiation when anywidget is missing.

docs.title: AnnotatedType
docs.text:
A callable type alias that preserves spytial annotations.

This provides a clean syntax for reusable annotated types:

# Define once
Graph = AnnotatedType(Dict[int, List[int]],
    InferredEdge(selector=EDGE_SEL, name=" "))

# Use naturally - Graph() wraps the value with type info
g = Graph({0: [1], 1: [2], 2: []})
diagram(g)  # Annotations applied automatically!

The AnnotatedType acts as both: - A type hint (for documentation/type checkers) - A constructor (wraps values with the type info)

docs.title: __init__(base_type, *annotations)
docs.text:
Create an annotated type alias.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default base_type Any

The underlying type (e.g., Dict[int, List[int]])

required *annotations Any

SpytialAnnotation instances (Orientation, InferredEdge, etc.)

()

docs.title: __call__(value)
docs.text:
Wrap a value with this type's annotations.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default value Any

The value to wrap

required

Returns:

Name Type Description Typed Typed

A wrapper that diagram() will recognize

docs.title: SpytialAnnotation
docs.text:
Base class for spytial type annotations.

Subclasses represent specific constraints or directives that can be attached to type aliases using typing.Annotated.

docs.title: to_entry()
docs.text: Convert to the internal registry format.

docs.title: Orientation
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Orientation constraint for spatial layout.

Usage

from typing import Annotated IntList = Annotated[list[int], Orientation(selector='items', directions=['horizontal'])]

docs.title: Cyclic
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Cyclic constraint for circular layouts.

Usage

NodeRing = Annotated[list[Node], Cyclic(selector='items', direction='clockwise')]

docs.title: Align
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Alignment constraint.

Usage

AlignedList = Annotated[list[int], Align(selector='items', direction='left')]

docs.title: Group
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Grouping constraint.

Usage (field-based): Tree = Annotated[TreeNode, Group(field='children', groupOn=0, addToGroup=1)]

Usage (selector-based): Grouped = Annotated[MyType, Group(selector='items', name='mygroup')]

docs.title: AtomColor
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Atom color directive.

Usage

ColoredList = Annotated[list[int], AtomColor(selector='self', value='blue')]

docs.title: Size
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Size directive.

Usage

SizedList = Annotated[list[int], Size(selector='items', height=50, width=50)]

docs.title: Icon
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Icon directive.

Usage

IconList = Annotated[list[int], Icon(selector='items', path='icon.svg', showLabels=True)]

docs.title: EdgeColor
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Edge color directive.

Usage

ColoredEdges = Annotated[Tree, EdgeColor(field='children', value='red')] StyledEdges = Annotated[Tree, EdgeColor(field='Uses', value='#d10000', style='dashed', weight=3, showLabel=False)] FilteredEdges = Annotated[Tree, EdgeColor(field='link', value='blue', filter='link & Active')] HiddenEdges = Annotated[Tree, EdgeColor(field='internal', value='gray', hidden=True)]

docs.title: HideField
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Hide field directive.

Usage

CleanView = Annotated[MyClass, HideField(field='_private')] Filtered = Annotated[MyClass, HideField(field='debug', filter='debug & Production')]

docs.title: HideAtom
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Hide atom directive.

Usage

Filtered = Annotated[list[int], HideAtom(selector='hidden')]

docs.title: Projection
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Projection directive.

Usage

Projected = Annotated[MyType, Projection(sig='MySig')]

docs.title: Attribute
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Attribute directive.

Usage

WithAttr = Annotated[MyType, Attribute(field='value')] Filtered = Annotated[MyType, Attribute(field='status', filter='status & Active')]

docs.title: InferredEdge
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Inferred edge directive.

Usage

WithEdges = Annotated[Graph, InferredEdge(name='connection', selector='nodes')] StyledEdges = Annotated[Graph, InferredEdge(name='ancestor', selector='^parent', color='gray', style='dotted', weight=2)]

docs.title: Tag
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Tag directive for adding computed attributes to nodes.

Unlike 'attribute', this does NOT remove edges - it adds computed attribute values to nodes based on selector evaluation.

Usage

docs.title: Simple binary tag - displays as "age: 25"
docs.text: Tagged = Annotated[MyType, Tag(toTag='Person', name='age', value='age')]

docs.title: Ternary selector - displays as "score[Math]: 95"
docs.text: Graded = Annotated[MyType, Tag(toTag='Student', name='score', value='grades')]

docs.title: Flag
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Flag directive.

Usage

Flagged = Annotated[MyType, Flag(name='debug')]

docs.title: to_entry()
docs.text: Flags store just the name as a scalar.

docs.title: relationalizer(cls=None, *, priority=0)
docs.text:
Decorator to register a class as a relationalizer.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default priority int

Higher priority relationalizers are tried first. Priorities 0-99 are reserved for built-in relationalizers.

0

docs.title: diagram(obj, method=None, auto_open=True, width=None, height=None, title=None, perf_path=None, perf_iterations=None, headless=False, timeout=None, as_type=None)
docs.text:
Display a Python object in the sPyTial visualizer.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default obj Any

Any Python object to visualize.

required method Optional[str]

Display method - "inline" (Jupyter), "browser" (new tab), "file" (save to file), or "headless" (headless browser for testing) If None (default), automatically selects "inline" in notebooks or "browser" otherwise.

None auto_open bool

Whether to automatically open browser (for "browser" method)

True width Optional[int]

Width of the visualization container in pixels (default: auto-detected)

None height Optional[int]

Height of the visualization container in pixels (default: auto-detected)

None title Optional[str]

Title for the browser tab/page (default: "sPyTial Visualization")

None perf_path Optional[Union[str, Path]]

Optional path to save performance metrics JSON file. If None, metrics are not saved (unless perf_iterations is set).

None perf_iterations Optional[int]

Optional number of times to render for performance benchmarking. If provided, the visualization will be rendered N times and metrics will be aggregated. Works with method="browser", "file", or "headless".

None headless bool

Run in headless Chrome for testing/benchmarking (requires selenium and chromedriver). If True, method is automatically set to "headless".

False timeout Optional[float]

Timeout in seconds for headless mode. If None, automatically calculated based on perf_iterations (default: max(120, perf_iterations * 5)). For large/complex visualizations, set this higher (e.g., timeout=600 for 10 minutes).

None as_type Optional[Any]

Optional annotated type to treat the object as. Annotations from this type will be applied in addition to any introspected from the object itself. Use AnnotatedType() or typing.Annotated to define types with spytial annotations.

None

Examples:

docs.title: Define an annotated type
docs.text: Graph = AnnotatedType(Dict[int, List[int]], InferredEdge(...), Orientation(...))

docs.title: Display a dict as a Graph
docs.text:
g = {0: [1], 1: [2]} diagram(g, as_type=Graph)

Returns:

Name Type Description str Optional[Union[str, Dict[str, Any]]]

Path to the generated HTML file (if method="file", "browser", or "headless")

dict Optional[Union[str, Dict[str, Any]]]

Performance metrics (if method="headless" and perf_iterations > 0)

docs.title: sequence(identity=None, sequence_policy='stability', method=None, auto_open=True, width=None, height=None, title=None, as_type=None)
docs.text:
Create a :class:SequenceRecorder for capturing algorithm steps or temporal snapshots.

The recorder uses a shared builder so atom IDs are stable across every :meth:~SequenceRecorder.record call within the same session.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default identity Optional[Callable[[Any], Optional[str]]]

Optional callable (obj) -> str | None. When provided, any object for which it returns a non-None string will receive an atom ID of identity:<returned-string>. Use this for deepcopy / snapshot workflows where Python's id() changes each frame but each node has a stable semantic key (e.g. a node's .id field).

None sequence_policy str

Continuity policy for the frontend renderer. One of "stability" (default), "ignore_history", "change_emphasis", or "random_positioning".

'stability' method Optional[str]

Display method — "inline" (Jupyter), "browser", or "file". Defaults to auto-detection.

None auto_open bool

Whether to open the browser automatically ("browser" method).

True width Optional[int]

Width of the visualization in pixels.

None height Optional[int]

Height of the visualization in pixels.

None title Optional[str]

Browser tab / page title.

None as_type Optional[Any]

Optional annotated type applied to every recorded object.

None

Returns:

Name Type Description A SequenceRecorder

class:SequenceRecorder instance, usable as a context manager.

Example::

with spytial.sequence() as seq:
    seq.record(data)
    step(data)
    seq.record(data)
seq.diagram()

docs.title: evaluate(obj, method=None, auto_open=True, width=None, height=None, as_type=None)
docs.text:
Evaluate a Python object using the sPyTial evaluator.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default obj Any

Any Python object to evaluate.

required method Optional[str]

Display method - "inline" (Jupyter), "browser" (new tab), or "file" (save to file). If None (default), automatically selects "inline" in notebooks or "browser" otherwise.

None auto_open bool

Whether to automatically open the browser (for "browser" method).

True width Optional[int]

Width of the evaluation container in pixels (default: auto-detected).

None height Optional[int]

Height of the evaluation container in pixels (default: auto-detected).

None as_type Optional[Any]

Optional annotated type to treat the object as. Annotations from this type will be applied in addition to any introspected from the object itself.

None

Returns:

Name Type Description str Optional[str]

Path to the generated HTML file (if method="file" or "browser").

docs.title: extract_spytial_annotations(type_hint)
docs.text:
Extract spytial annotations from a typing.Annotated type hint.

:param type_hint: A type hint, possibly Annotated with spytial markers. :return: A dict with 'constraints' and 'directives' lists, or None if no annotations.

docs.title: get_base_type(type_hint)
docs.text:
Get the base type from an Annotated type hint.

:param type_hint: A type hint, possibly Annotated. :return: The base type (unwrapped from Annotated if applicable).

docs.title: annotate_type_alias(type_alias, annotation_type, **kwargs)
docs.text:
Attach a spytial annotation to a type alias.

This function allows you to annotate type aliases (e.g., list[int], MyTypeAlias) with spatial constraints and directives. When objects are visualized, if their type annotation matches a registered type alias, these annotations are applied.

Usage

:param type_alias: The type alias to annotate (e.g., list[int], MyClass, etc.) :param annotation_type: The type of annotation ('orientation', 'group', etc.) :param kwargs: The annotation parameters. :return: The type alias (for chaining).

docs.title: Define a type alias
docs.text: IntList = list[int]

docs.title: Annotate it
docs.text: annotate_type_alias(IntList, 'orientation', selector='items', directions=['horizontal']) annotate_type_alias(IntList, 'atomColor', selector='self', value='blue')

docs.title: Or use the convenience functions:
docs.text: annotate_type_alias_orientation(IntList, selector='items', directions=['horizontal'])

docs.title: get_type_alias_annotations(type_alias)
docs.text:
Retrieve annotations registered for a type alias.

:param type_alias: The type alias to look up. :return: A dictionary with 'constraints' and 'directives' lists, or None if not found.

docs.title: clear_type_alias_annotations(type_alias=None)
docs.text:
Clear annotations for a specific type alias or all type aliases.

Note: This only clears the legacy registry-based annotations. Annotations using typing.Annotated are immutable and don't need clearing.

:param type_alias: If provided, clear only this type alias's annotations. If None, clear all type alias annotations.

docs.title: list_type_alias_annotations()
docs.text:
List all registered type alias annotations (legacy registry).

:return: A dictionary mapping type alias keys to their annotation registries.

docs.title: dont_inherit_constraints(cls)
docs.text:
Mark a class to not inherit constraints from parent classes.

This is useful when you want to override a parent class's constraints. Constraints applied directly to this class are still included.

Example

@orientation(selector='children', directions=['below']) class Parent: pass

@dont_inherit_constraints class Child(Parent): # Will NOT have Parent's orientation constraint pass

:param cls: The class to mark as not inheriting constraints. :return: The class (for chaining).

docs.title: dont_inherit_directives(cls)
docs.text:
Mark a class to not inherit directives from parent classes.

This is useful when you want to override a parent class's directives. Directives applied directly to this class are still included.

Example

@atomColor(selector='root', value='red') class Parent: pass

@dont_inherit_directives class Child(Parent): # Will NOT have Parent's atomColor directive pass

:param cls: The class to mark as not inheriting directives. :return: The class (for chaining).

docs.title: dont_inherit_annotations(cls)
docs.text:
Mark a class to not inherit any annotations from parent classes.

Shorthand for applying both dont_inherit_constraints and dont_inherit_directives.

Example

@orientation(selector='children', directions=['below']) @atomColor(selector='root', value='red') class Parent: pass

@dont_inherit_annotations class Child(Parent): # Will NOT have any annotations from Parent pass

:param cls: The class to mark as not inheriting annotations. :return: The class (for chaining).

docs.title: collect_decorators(obj, type_hint=None)
docs.text: Collect all decorators applied to the class of the given object, as well as any annotations applied directly to the object instance, and any annotations registered for matching type aliases. Respects inheritance control flags (dont_inherit_constraints, dont_inherit_directives). :param obj: The object whose class decorators and object annotations should be collected. :param type_hint: Optional type hint to look up type alias annotations. :return: A combined dictionary of constraints and directives (deduplicated).

docs.title: serialize_to_yaml_string(decorators)
docs.text: Serialize the collected constraints and directives to a YAML string. :param decorators: The collected decorators (constraints and directives). :return: YAML string representation of the decorators.

docs.title: reset_object_ids()
docs.text:
Reset the global object ID state. Useful for testing or when you need deterministic object ID generation across multiple runs.

Warning: This will clear all existing object ID mappings, so existing selectors that depend on previous object IDs may no longer work.

docs.title: apply_if(condition, *decorators)
docs.text: Conditional decorator macro for sPyTial. Usage: @apply_if(CONDITION, orientation(...), hideField(...), attribute(...), ... ) class MyClass: ... If CONDITION is True, applies all decorators in order to the class. If CONDITION is False, returns the class unchanged.

docs.title: spytial.visualizer
docs.text:
sPyTial Visualization Display Module

This module provides functions to display Python objects using the sPyTial visualizer.

docs.title: SequenceRecorder
docs.text:
Context manager for recording a sequence of object snapshots for visualization.

Uses a single shared builder across all :meth:record calls so that atom IDs remain stable across frames. This handles two common patterns:

  • In-place mutation — the same Python objects are modified between frames. Because id(obj) never changes, the persistent ID table keeps IDs stable automatically with no extra configuration.

  • Snapshot / deepcopy — a fresh copy of the data structure is created each step (e.g. functional algorithms, undo-history). Pass an identity callable that returns a stable string key for each object; atoms will then receive an identity:<key> ID that stays constant across frames.

Example — in-place mutation::

with spytial.sequence() as seq:
    seq.record(root)
    insert(root, 42)
    seq.record(root)
    insert(root, 7)
    seq.record(root)
seq.diagram()

Example — deepcopy snapshots with identity::

with spytial.sequence(identity=lambda n: str(n.id)) as seq:
    for snap in snapshots:
        seq.record(snap)
seq.diagram(method="file")

docs.title: record(obj)
docs.text: Capture a snapshot of obj as the next frame in the sequence.

docs.title: diagram(method=None, auto_open=None, width=None, height=None, title=None, sequence_policy=None)
docs.text:
Render all recorded frames as an interactive sequence visualization.

Parameters override the values supplied to :func:sequence / the constructor; omit them to use the constructor defaults.

docs.title: diagram(obj, method=None, auto_open=True, width=None, height=None, title=None, perf_path=None, perf_iterations=None, headless=False, timeout=None, as_type=None)
docs.text:
Display a Python object in the sPyTial visualizer.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default obj Any

Any Python object to visualize.

required method Optional[str]

Display method - "inline" (Jupyter), "browser" (new tab), "file" (save to file), or "headless" (headless browser for testing) If None (default), automatically selects "inline" in notebooks or "browser" otherwise.

None auto_open bool

Whether to automatically open browser (for "browser" method)

True width Optional[int]

Width of the visualization container in pixels (default: auto-detected)

None height Optional[int]

Height of the visualization container in pixels (default: auto-detected)

None title Optional[str]

Title for the browser tab/page (default: "sPyTial Visualization")

None perf_path Optional[Union[str, Path]]

Optional path to save performance metrics JSON file. If None, metrics are not saved (unless perf_iterations is set).

None perf_iterations Optional[int]

Optional number of times to render for performance benchmarking. If provided, the visualization will be rendered N times and metrics will be aggregated. Works with method="browser", "file", or "headless".

None headless bool

Run in headless Chrome for testing/benchmarking (requires selenium and chromedriver). If True, method is automatically set to "headless".

False timeout Optional[float]

Timeout in seconds for headless mode. If None, automatically calculated based on perf_iterations (default: max(120, perf_iterations * 5)). For large/complex visualizations, set this higher (e.g., timeout=600 for 10 minutes).

None as_type Optional[Any]

Optional annotated type to treat the object as. Annotations from this type will be applied in addition to any introspected from the object itself. Use AnnotatedType() or typing.Annotated to define types with spytial annotations.

None

Examples:

docs.title: Define an annotated type
docs.text: Graph = AnnotatedType(Dict[int, List[int]], InferredEdge(...), Orientation(...))

docs.title: Display a dict as a Graph
docs.text:
g = {0: [1], 1: [2]} diagram(g, as_type=Graph)

Returns:

Name Type Description str Optional[Union[str, Dict[str, Any]]]

Path to the generated HTML file (if method="file", "browser", or "headless")

dict Optional[Union[str, Dict[str, Any]]]

Performance metrics (if method="headless" and perf_iterations > 0)

docs.title: sequence(identity=None, sequence_policy='stability', method=None, auto_open=True, width=None, height=None, title=None, as_type=None)
docs.text:
Create a :class:SequenceRecorder for capturing algorithm steps or temporal snapshots.

The recorder uses a shared builder so atom IDs are stable across every :meth:~SequenceRecorder.record call within the same session.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default identity Optional[Callable[[Any], Optional[str]]]

Optional callable (obj) -> str | None. When provided, any object for which it returns a non-None string will receive an atom ID of identity:<returned-string>. Use this for deepcopy / snapshot workflows where Python's id() changes each frame but each node has a stable semantic key (e.g. a node's .id field).

None sequence_policy str

Continuity policy for the frontend renderer. One of "stability" (default), "ignore_history", "change_emphasis", or "random_positioning".

'stability' method Optional[str]

Display method — "inline" (Jupyter), "browser", or "file". Defaults to auto-detection.

None auto_open bool

Whether to open the browser automatically ("browser" method).

True width Optional[int]

Width of the visualization in pixels.

None height Optional[int]

Height of the visualization in pixels.

None title Optional[str]

Browser tab / page title.

None as_type Optional[Any]

Optional annotated type applied to every recorded object.

None

Returns:

Name Type Description A SequenceRecorder

class:SequenceRecorder instance, usable as a context manager.

Example::

with spytial.sequence() as seq:
    seq.record(data)
    step(data)
    seq.record(data)
seq.diagram()

docs.title: spytial.evaluator
docs.text:
sPyTial Evaluator Module

This module provides functions to evaluate expressions using the sPyTial evaluator.

docs.title: evaluate(obj, method=None, auto_open=True, width=None, height=None, as_type=None)
docs.text:
Evaluate a Python object using the sPyTial evaluator.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default obj Any

Any Python object to evaluate.

required method Optional[str]

Display method - "inline" (Jupyter), "browser" (new tab), or "file" (save to file). If None (default), automatically selects "inline" in notebooks or "browser" otherwise.

None auto_open bool

Whether to automatically open the browser (for "browser" method).

True width Optional[int]

Width of the evaluation container in pixels (default: auto-detected).

None height Optional[int]

Height of the evaluation container in pixels (default: auto-detected).

None as_type Optional[Any]

Optional annotated type to treat the object as. Annotations from this type will be applied in addition to any introspected from the object itself.

None

Returns:

Name Type Description str Optional[str]

Path to the generated HTML file (if method="file" or "browser").

docs.title: spytial.dataclass_builder
docs.text:
sPyTial DataClassBuilder

Two paths for visual dataclass editing:

  • Widget path – :class:DataClassBuilder is an anywidget-based Jupyter widget with live two-way sync via traitlets. Requires an IPython kernel (standard Jupyter).

  • HTML path – :func:dataclass_builder renders standalone HTML via input_template.html and displays it with an inline iframe or browser tab, exactly like :func:diagram. No kernel needed – works in pyodide and other lightweight environments.

docs.title: DataClassBuilder
docs.text: Placeholder that raises on instantiation when anywidget is missing.

docs.title: dataclass_builder(instance, *, method=None, auto_open=True, height=500)
docs.text:
Render a visual dataclass builder as standalone HTML.

This is the pyodide-compatible path. It generates a full HTML page with the <structured-input-graph> editor and displays it via an inline iframe (in notebooks) or a browser tab. Use the built-in Export button to copy the resulting Python constructor code.

For live two-way sync in Jupyter, use :class:DataClassBuilder instead.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default instance Any

A dataclass instance to start editing from.

required method Optional[str]

"inline" (default in notebooks), "browser", or None (auto-detect).

None auto_open bool

Open the browser tab automatically (only for method="browser").

True height int

Pixel height of the inline iframe.

500

Returns:

Type Description Optional[str]

None when displayed inline, or the path to the temp HTML file

Optional[str]

when displayed in a browser.

Example::

spytial.dataclass_builder(TreeNode(value=1))

docs.title: SpytialAnnotation
docs.text:
Base class for spytial type annotations.

Subclasses represent specific constraints or directives that can be attached to type aliases using typing.Annotated.

docs.title: to_entry()
docs.text: Convert to the internal registry format.

docs.title: Orientation
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Orientation constraint for spatial layout.

Usage

from typing import Annotated IntList = Annotated[list[int], Orientation(selector='items', directions=['horizontal'])]

docs.title: Cyclic
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Cyclic constraint for circular layouts.

Usage

NodeRing = Annotated[list[Node], Cyclic(selector='items', direction='clockwise')]

docs.title: Align
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Alignment constraint.

Usage

AlignedList = Annotated[list[int], Align(selector='items', direction='left')]

docs.title: Group
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Grouping constraint.

Usage (field-based): Tree = Annotated[TreeNode, Group(field='children', groupOn=0, addToGroup=1)]

Usage (selector-based): Grouped = Annotated[MyType, Group(selector='items', name='mygroup')]

docs.title: AtomColor
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Atom color directive.

Usage

ColoredList = Annotated[list[int], AtomColor(selector='self', value='blue')]

docs.title: Size
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Size directive.

Usage

SizedList = Annotated[list[int], Size(selector='items', height=50, width=50)]

docs.title: Icon
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Icon directive.

Usage

IconList = Annotated[list[int], Icon(selector='items', path='icon.svg', showLabels=True)]

docs.title: EdgeColor
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Edge color directive.

Usage

ColoredEdges = Annotated[Tree, EdgeColor(field='children', value='red')] StyledEdges = Annotated[Tree, EdgeColor(field='Uses', value='#d10000', style='dashed', weight=3, showLabel=False)] FilteredEdges = Annotated[Tree, EdgeColor(field='link', value='blue', filter='link & Active')] HiddenEdges = Annotated[Tree, EdgeColor(field='internal', value='gray', hidden=True)]

docs.title: HideField
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Hide field directive.

Usage

CleanView = Annotated[MyClass, HideField(field='_private')] Filtered = Annotated[MyClass, HideField(field='debug', filter='debug & Production')]

docs.title: HideAtom
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Hide atom directive.

Usage

Filtered = Annotated[list[int], HideAtom(selector='hidden')]

docs.title: Projection
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Projection directive.

Usage

Projected = Annotated[MyType, Projection(sig='MySig')]

docs.title: Attribute
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Attribute directive.

Usage

WithAttr = Annotated[MyType, Attribute(field='value')] Filtered = Annotated[MyType, Attribute(field='status', filter='status & Active')]

docs.title: InferredEdge
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Inferred edge directive.

Usage

WithEdges = Annotated[Graph, InferredEdge(name='connection', selector='nodes')] StyledEdges = Annotated[Graph, InferredEdge(name='ancestor', selector='^parent', color='gray', style='dotted', weight=2)]

docs.title: Flag
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Flag directive.

Usage

Flagged = Annotated[MyType, Flag(name='debug')]

docs.title: to_entry()
docs.text: Flags store just the name as a scalar.

docs.title: Tag
docs.text:
Bases: SpytialAnnotation

Tag directive for adding computed attributes to nodes.

Unlike 'attribute', this does NOT remove edges - it adds computed attribute values to nodes based on selector evaluation.

Usage

docs.title: Simple binary tag - displays as "age: 25"
docs.text: Tagged = Annotated[MyType, Tag(toTag='Person', name='age', value='age')]

docs.title: Ternary selector - displays as "score[Math]: 95"
docs.text: Graded = Annotated[MyType, Tag(toTag='Student', name='score', value='grades')]

docs.title: extract_spytial_annotations(type_hint)
docs.text:
Extract spytial annotations from a typing.Annotated type hint.

:param type_hint: A type hint, possibly Annotated with spytial markers. :return: A dict with 'constraints' and 'directives' lists, or None if no annotations.

docs.title: get_base_type(type_hint)
docs.text:
Get the base type from an Annotated type hint.

:param type_hint: A type hint, possibly Annotated. :return: The base type (unwrapped from Annotated if applicable).

docs.title: annotate_type_alias(type_alias, annotation_type, **kwargs)
docs.text:
Attach a spytial annotation to a type alias.

This function allows you to annotate type aliases (e.g., list[int], MyTypeAlias) with spatial constraints and directives. When objects are visualized, if their type annotation matches a registered type alias, these annotations are applied.

Usage

:param type_alias: The type alias to annotate (e.g., list[int], MyClass, etc.) :param annotation_type: The type of annotation ('orientation', 'group', etc.) :param kwargs: The annotation parameters. :return: The type alias (for chaining).

docs.title: Define a type alias
docs.text: IntList = list[int]

docs.title: Annotate it
docs.text: annotate_type_alias(IntList, 'orientation', selector='items', directions=['horizontal']) annotate_type_alias(IntList, 'atomColor', selector='self', value='blue')

docs.title: Or use the convenience functions:
docs.text: annotate_type_alias_orientation(IntList, selector='items', directions=['horizontal'])

docs.title: get_type_alias_annotations(type_alias)
docs.text:
Retrieve annotations registered for a type alias.

:param type_alias: The type alias to look up. :return: A dictionary with 'constraints' and 'directives' lists, or None if not found.

docs.title: clear_type_alias_annotations(type_alias=None)
docs.text:
Clear annotations for a specific type alias or all type aliases.

Note: This only clears the legacy registry-based annotations. Annotations using typing.Annotated are immutable and don't need clearing.

:param type_alias: If provided, clear only this type alias's annotations. If None, clear all type alias annotations.

docs.title: list_type_alias_annotations()
docs.text:
List all registered type alias annotations (legacy registry).

:return: A dictionary mapping type alias keys to their annotation registries.

docs.title: reset_object_ids()
docs.text:
Reset the global object ID state. Useful for testing or when you need deterministic object ID generation across multiple runs.

Warning: This will clear all existing object ID mappings, so existing selectors that depend on previous object IDs may no longer work.

docs.title: validate_fields(type_, kwargs, valid_fields)
docs.text: Validate that the required fields for a given type are present in kwargs. For constraints that support multiple parameter sets (like group), try each set. :param type_: The type of constraint or directive. :param kwargs: The provided fields for the decorator. :param valid_fields: The list of required fields for the type, or a list of alternative field sets. :raises ValueError: If no valid field set matches the provided kwargs.

docs.title: annotate_orientation(obj, **kwargs)
docs.text: Apply orientation annotation to a specific object.

docs.title: annotate_cyclic(obj, **kwargs)
docs.text: Apply cyclic annotation to a specific object.

docs.title: annotate_align(obj, **kwargs)
docs.text: Apply align annotation to a specific object.

docs.title: annotate_group(obj, **kwargs)
docs.text: Apply group annotation to a specific object.

docs.title: annotate_atomColor(obj, **kwargs)
docs.text: Apply atomColor annotation to a specific object.

docs.text: Apply size annotation to a specific object.

docs.title: annotate_icon(obj, **kwargs)
docs.text: Apply icon annotation to a specific object.

docs.title: annotate_edgeColor(obj, **kwargs)
docs.text: Apply edgeColor annotation to a specific object.

docs.title: annotate_projection(obj, **kwargs)
docs.text: Apply projection annotation to a specific object.

docs.title: annotate_attribute(obj, **kwargs)
docs.text: Apply attribute annotation to a specific object.

docs.text: Apply hideField annotation to a specific object.

docs.title: annotate_hideAtom(obj, **kwargs)
docs.text: Apply hideAtom annotation to a specific object.

docs.title: annotate_inferredEdge(obj, **kwargs)
docs.text: Apply inferredEdge annotation to a specific object.

docs.title: annotate_tag(obj, **kwargs)
docs.text: Apply tag annotation to a specific object.

docs.title: annotate_flag(obj, **kwargs)
docs.text: Apply flag annotation to a specific object.

docs.title: annotate(obj, annotation_type, **kwargs)
docs.text: Apply any annotation type to a specific object. :param obj: The object to annotate. :param annotation_type: The type of annotation (e.g., 'orientation', 'cyclic'). :param kwargs: The annotation parameters. :return: The annotated object (for chaining).

docs.title: apply_if(condition, *decorators)
docs.text: Conditional decorator macro for sPyTial. Usage: @apply_if(CONDITION, orientation(...), hideField(...), attribute(...), ... ) class MyClass: ... If CONDITION is True, applies all decorators in order to the class. If CONDITION is False, returns the class unchanged.

docs.title: collect_decorators(obj, type_hint=None)
docs.text: Collect all decorators applied to the class of the given object, as well as any annotations applied directly to the object instance, and any annotations registered for matching type aliases. Respects inheritance control flags (dont_inherit_constraints, dont_inherit_directives). :param obj: The object whose class decorators and object annotations should be collected. :param type_hint: Optional type hint to look up type alias annotations. :return: A combined dictionary of constraints and directives (deduplicated).

docs.title: serialize_to_yaml_string(decorators)
docs.text: Serialize the collected constraints and directives to a YAML string. :param decorators: The collected decorators (constraints and directives). :return: YAML string representation of the decorators.

docs.title: dont_inherit_constraints(cls)
docs.text:
Mark a class to not inherit constraints from parent classes.

This is useful when you want to override a parent class's constraints. Constraints applied directly to this class are still included.

Example

@orientation(selector='children', directions=['below']) class Parent: pass

@dont_inherit_constraints class Child(Parent): # Will NOT have Parent's orientation constraint pass

:param cls: The class to mark as not inheriting constraints. :return: The class (for chaining).

docs.title: dont_inherit_directives(cls)
docs.text:
Mark a class to not inherit directives from parent classes.

This is useful when you want to override a parent class's directives. Directives applied directly to this class are still included.

Example

@atomColor(selector='root', value='red') class Parent: pass

@dont_inherit_directives class Child(Parent): # Will NOT have Parent's atomColor directive pass

:param cls: The class to mark as not inheriting directives. :return: The class (for chaining).

docs.title: dont_inherit_annotations(cls)
docs.text:
Mark a class to not inherit any annotations from parent classes.

Shorthand for applying both dont_inherit_constraints and dont_inherit_directives.

Example

@orientation(selector='children', directions=['below']) @atomColor(selector='root', value='red') class Parent: pass

@dont_inherit_annotations class Child(Parent): # Will NOT have any annotations from Parent pass

:param cls: The class to mark as not inheriting annotations. :return: The class (for chaining).

docs.title: spytial.provider_system
docs.text:
Spytial-Core Data Instance Relationalizer Architecture

This module provides a pluggable system for converting Python objects into CnD-compatible atom/relation representations using relationalizers.

docs.title: RelationalizerRegistry
docs.text: Registry for relationalizers.

docs.title: register(relationalizer_cls, priority=0) classmethod
docs.text: Register a relationalizer class with given priority.

docs.title: find_relationalizer(obj) classmethod
docs.text: Find the first relationalizer that can handle the given object.

docs.title: list_relationalizers() classmethod
docs.text:
List all registered relationalizers with their priorities.

Returns:

Type Description List[Tuple[int, str]]

List of (priority, relationalizer_class_name) tuples, sorted by priority (highest first)

docs.title: clear() classmethod
docs.text: Clear all registered relationalizers (for testing).

docs.title: CnDDataInstanceBuilder
docs.text: Main builder that composes providers to build data instances.

docs.title: build_instance(obj, as_type=None)
docs.text:
Build a complete data instance from an object.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default obj Any

The Python object to build an instance from.

required as_type Optional[Any]

Optional annotated type to treat the object as. Annotations from this type will be applied in addition to introspected ones.

None

docs.title: get_collected_decorators()
docs.text:
Get all decorators collected during the build process, deduplicated.

During _walk(), the same class-level decorators are collected once per object instance of that class. This deduplicates them so the final YAML spec contains each unique rule only once.

docs.title: __call__(obj)
docs.text: Allow the builder to be called as a function for recursive walking.

docs.title: build_types(atoms)
docs.text:
Build the types field for the data instance.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default atoms List[Dict]

List of atoms, each containing a "type" field with the most specific type.

required

Returns:

Type Description List[Dict]

List of type definitions, each matching the desired format.

docs.title: reify(data_instance)
docs.text:
Reconstruct Python objects from a Spytial-Core data instance.

This method reverses the build_instance operation, converting atoms and relations back into Python objects.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default data_instance Dict

Dictionary containing 'atoms', 'relations', and 'types' as returned by build_instance()

required

Returns:

Type Description Any

The reconstructed Python object corresponding to the root atom

Raises:

Type Description ValueError

If the data instance is malformed or cannot be reconstructed

docs.title: can_reify(data_instance)
docs.text:
Check if a data instance can be reified.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default data_instance Dict

Dictionary to check

required

Returns:

Type Description bool

True if the data instance appears to be valid for reification

docs.title: register_reifier(type_name, reifier_func)
docs.text:
Register a custom reifier function for a specific type.

This provides an extensibility mechanism for complex reconstruction scenarios.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default type_name str

The atom type name to handle (e.g., "MyCustomClass")

required reifier_func Callable[..., Any]

Function with signature (atom, relations, reify_atom) -> object where: - atom: Dict containing atom information (id, type, label, etc.) - relations: Dict mapping relation names to lists of target atom IDs - reify_atom: Function to recursively reify other atoms by ID

required Example

def custom_reifier(atom, relations, reify_atom): # Custom reconstruction logic obj = MyCustomClass() for rel_name, target_ids in relations.items(): setattr(obj, rel_name, [reify_atom(tid) for tid in target_ids]) return obj

builder.register_reifier("MyCustomClass", custom_reifier)

docs.title: unregister_reifier(type_name)
docs.text:
Remove a custom reifier for a specific type.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default type_name str

The atom type name to remove the reifier for

required

docs.title: list_reifiers()
docs.text:
List all registered custom reifier type names.

Returns:

Type Description List[str]

List of type names that have custom reifiers registered

docs.title: relationalizer(cls=None, *, priority=0)
docs.text:
Decorator to register a class as a relationalizer.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default priority int

Higher priority relationalizers are tried first. Priorities 0-99 are reserved for built-in relationalizers.

0

docs.title: spytial.utils
docs.text: Shared utilities for sPyTial.

docs.title: Typed
docs.text:
A wrapper that preserves type hint information for values.

Use this when you want to pass annotated type information to diagram() without explicitly passing type_hint=.

Example

Graph = Annotated[Dict[int, List[int]], Orientation(...)] g = {0: [1], 1: [2]} diagram(Typed(g, Graph)) # Annotations from Graph will be applied

docs.title: AnnotatedType
docs.text:
A callable type alias that preserves spytial annotations.

This provides a clean syntax for reusable annotated types:

# Define once
Graph = AnnotatedType(Dict[int, List[int]],
    InferredEdge(selector=EDGE_SEL, name=" "))

# Use naturally - Graph() wraps the value with type info
g = Graph({0: [1], 1: [2], 2: []})
diagram(g)  # Annotations applied automatically!

The AnnotatedType acts as both: - A type hint (for documentation/type checkers) - A constructor (wraps values with the type info)

docs.title: __init__(base_type, *annotations)
docs.text:
Create an annotated type alias.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default base_type Any

The underlying type (e.g., Dict[int, List[int]])

required *annotations Any

SpytialAnnotation instances (Orientation, InferredEdge, etc.)

()

docs.title: __call__(value)
docs.text:
Wrap a value with this type's annotations.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default value Any

The value to wrap

required

Returns:

Name Type Description Typed Typed

A wrapper that diagram() will recognize

docs.title: is_notebook()
docs.text: Detect if we're running in a Jupyter notebook environment. Returns True if in a notebook, False otherwise.

docs.title: default_method()
docs.text: Return the default display method based on environment. Returns 'inline' in notebooks, 'browser' otherwise.

docs.title: Dataclass Builder
docs.text: spytial.dataclass_builder() opens an interactive builder for a dataclass instance. It lets you visually edit the structure and export the resulting constructor code.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional
import spytial

@dataclass
class TreeNode:
    value: int = 0
    left: Optional["TreeNode"] = None
    right: Optional["TreeNode"] = None

# Start with an empty instance
spytial.dataclass_builder(TreeNode())

docs.title: What it expects
docs.text: The builder requires a dataclass instance, not a class object. Pass TreeNode() rather than TreeNode.

docs.title: Output methods
docs.text:
spytial.dataclass_builder(TreeNode(), method="browser")
spytial.dataclass_builder(TreeNode(), method="file")
spytial.dataclass_builder(TreeNode(), method="inline")

docs.title: Typical workflow
docs.text:
1. Start with a minimal dataclass instance.
  2. Open the builder.
  3. Edit the structure in the UI.
  4. Use Export to copy the generated constructor code back into your Python code.

Tip

For large models, start with a minimal instance and build up visually before exporting the final constructor.

docs.title: Diagramming
docs.text: spytial.diagram() turns Python objects into a spatial diagram. It works with built-in containers, dataclasses, custom classes, and graph-like structures.

docs.title: Basic usage
docs.text:
import spytial

spytial.diagram({"a": 1, "b": [1, 2, 3]})

docs.title: Display options
docs.text:
spytial.diagram(data, method="browser")  # open a new tab
spytial.diagram(data, method="file")     # save as spytial_visualization.html
spytial.diagram(data, method="inline")   # force notebook output

docs.title: Sizing and title
docs.text: spytial.diagram(data, width=900, height=600, title="My Diagram")

docs.title: Passing annotations with as_type
docs.text:
Use AnnotatedType or typing.Annotated to attach spatial constraints to a type you want the object treated as.

from typing import Dict, List
from spytial import AnnotatedType, InferredEdge, Orientation, Tag

Graph = AnnotatedType(
    Dict[int, List[int]],
    InferredEdge(name="edge", selector="values"),
    Orientation(selector="values", directions=["right"]),
    Tag(toTag="univ", name="count", value="values"),
)

graph = {0: [1, 2], 1: [3]}
spytial.diagram(graph, as_type=Graph)

docs.title: Common workflow
docs.text:
For non-trivial objects, the usual workflow is:

  1. spytial.evaluate(obj) to confirm the serialized atoms and relations.
  2. spytial.diagram(obj) to see the layout.
  3. Add decorators or AnnotatedType(...) annotations if you want more control over layout or styling.

docs.title: Headless benchmarking
docs.text:
For large structures, you can render multiple times and save aggregated timing.

import spytial

metrics = spytial.diagram(
    data,
    headless=True,
    perf_path="perf.json",
    perf_iterations=5,
)

Note

Headless mode requires Selenium and a Chrome/Chromedriver installation. Use pip install spytial_diagramming[headless] and ensure the driver is on your PATH.

docs.title: Return values
docs.text:
• method="browser" and method="file" return the HTML file path.
  • headless=True returns performance metrics when perf_iterations is set.

docs.title: Patterns from spytial-clrs
docs.text:
If you want realistic examples instead of toy snippets, use the notebooks in spytial-clrs:

  • linked-lists.ipynb shows list-like structures and cyclic constraints.
  • heaps.ipynb and trees.ipynb show recursive structures with alignment and attributes.
  • graphs.ipynb and disjoint-sets.ipynb show grouping-heavy layouts.

docs.title: Evaluator
docs.text: spytial.evaluate() renders a lightweight view of the serialized data instance. Use it when you want to check how Python objects are being translated before you worry about layout.

docs.title: Basic usage
docs.text:
import spytial

spytial.evaluate({"total": 3, "items": [1, 2, 3]})

docs.title: When it helps
docs.text:
The evaluator is especially useful when:

  • a custom class is not relationalizing the way you expect
  • an annotation selector is not matching what you think it is matching
  • you are writing a custom relationalizer and want to inspect the emitted atoms and relations

docs.title: Display methods
docs.text:
spytial.evaluate(data, method="browser")  # open a new tab
spytial.evaluate(data, method="file")     # save as cnd_evaluator.html
spytial.evaluate(data, method="inline")   # force notebook output

docs.title: Size options
docs.text: spytial.evaluate(data, width=800, height=300)

docs.title: Using annotated types
docs.text:
from typing import Dict, List
from spytial import AnnotatedType, InferredEdge, Orientation

Graph = AnnotatedType(
    Dict[int, List[int]],
    InferredEdge(name="edge", selector="values"),
    Orientation(selector="values", directions=["right"]),
)

spytial.evaluate({0: [1, 2], 1: [3]}, as_type=Graph)

docs.title: Recommended workflow
docs.text: Use evaluate() first when debugging serialization, then switch to diagram() once the data instance looks right.

docs.title: Sequences of Diagrams
docs.text:
spytial.sequence() lets you record a series of snapshots of a data structure and play them back as an interactive step-by-step visualization. It is designed for two use cases:

  • Algorithm tracing — call .record() inside an algorithm as each step completes.
  • Temporal snapshots — record a structure at multiple points in time.

docs.title: Basic usage
docs.text:
import spytial

with spytial.sequence(sequence_policy="stability") as seq:
    seq.record({"count": 0, "items": ["a"]})
    seq.record({"count": 1, "items": ["a", "b"]})
    seq.record({"count": 2, "items": ["b"]})
seq.diagram()

sequence() returns a SequenceRecorder. Each .record(obj) call captures the current state of obj as the next frame. .diagram() renders all frames into an interactive viewer with Previous / Next navigation.

docs.title: Sequence policies
docs.text:
The sequence_policy controls how the frontend positions atoms across frames:

Policy Behaviour stability Atoms stay anchored to their previous position (default for sequences) change_emphasis Changed atoms are visually highlighted ignore_history Each frame is laid out independently random_positioning Random placement each frame

docs.title: In-place mutation (most common)
docs.text:
When the same Python objects are mutated between frames, atom IDs are stable automatically — no configuration needed. The recorder uses a single shared builder whose persistent ID table survives across .record() calls.

class Node:
    def __init__(self, val, left=None, right=None):
        self.val = val
        self.left = left
        self.right = right

root = Node(5, Node(3), Node(7))

with spytial.sequence(sequence_policy="stability") as seq:
    seq.record(root)
    root.val = 99          # mutate in place
    seq.record(root)
    root.left.val = 11
    seq.record(root)
seq.diagram()

This pattern works well for BST insertion, sorting algorithms, graph BFS/DFS — any algorithm that operates on a shared mutable structure.

docs.title: Snapshot / deepcopy workflow
docs.text:
If each frame is a fresh object (e.g. copy.deepcopy), pass identity= so the recorder can match conceptually equivalent nodes across frames. The callable receives each object and should return a stable string key or None.

import copy, spytial

snapshots = [copy.deepcopy(root) for _ in range(3)]

with spytial.sequence(
    identity=lambda obj: str(obj.node_id) if hasattr(obj, "node_id") else None,
    sequence_policy="stability",
) as seq:
    for snap in snapshots:
        seq.record(snap)
seq.diagram()

identity must return a str or None. Objects that share the same key across frames are rendered as the same atom and animated smoothly between positions.

docs.title: Passing as_type
docs.text:
You can apply class-level spatial annotations to every recorded object via as_type:

seq = spytial.sequence(as_type=MyAnnotatedType, sequence_policy="stability")

docs.title: Display options
docs.text:
seq.diagram()                    # auto-detect (inline in Jupyter, browser otherwise)
seq.diagram(method="file")       # save as spytial_sequence_visualization.html
seq.diagram(method="browser")    # open in a new browser tab
seq.diagram(method="inline")     # force Jupyter inline output
seq.diagram(width=1200, height=800, title="My algorithm")

You can also set defaults at construction time and override at render time:

seq = spytial.sequence(method="file", auto_open=False)
# ... record frames ...
seq.diagram(title="Final render")

docs.title: Using without a context manager
docs.text:
SequenceRecorder does not require with. The context manager is purely a readability convention — __exit__ does nothing special.

seq = spytial.sequence(sequence_policy="stability")
tree.insert(seq, 42)   # tree passes seq around internally and calls seq.record()
seq.diagram()
